metadata.course_name: Stanford CS336 Language Modeling from Scratch

discussions.title: Introduction to GPUs and Lecture Goals
discussions.summary:
This section introduces GPUs and the goal of the lecture: to help you understand how GPUs work and how to speed up algorithms using them.

Definitions:
- GPU (Graphics Processing Unit): A specialized computer chip that helps run large language models fast by doing many calculations at once.
- CUDA: A programming system for writing software that runs on GPUs.
- Flash Attention 2: A new method to compute the attention operation efficiently in transformers.

Key Concepts:
- GPUs are very important for running language models because they do many calculations in parallel.
- The lecture will explain why GPU speed changes unpredictably for some tasks.
- Understanding GPUs helps you improve software like Flash Attention.
- The lecture focuses on one GPU at a time and briefly compares it to TPUs (another type of accelerator).

Important Notes:
- Understanding GPU hardware can seem hard but is essential for making your models run faster.
- The lecture draws on external resources like Horus Heath’s blog and Google’s TPU book for deeper learning.

discussions.title: Why Hardware Matters and CPU vs GPU Design
discussions.summary:
This part explains why faster hardware is key for training big language models, and compares CPUs and GPUs to show their different designs and goals.

Definitions:
- Scaling Laws: Rules that describe how model performance improves as compute grows.
- Moore’s Law: The idea that the number of transistors on chips doubles roughly every year.
- Dennard Scaling: Past trend where making transistors smaller allowed chips to run faster with less power.
- Latency: How fast a single task finishes.
- Throughput: How many tasks finish in total over time.

Key Concepts:
- Machine learning progress depends a lot on faster hardware and better ways to use it.
- CPUs focus on finishing single tasks quickly (low latency) using complex control logic.
- GPUs focus on doing many tasks at once (high throughput) by having many simple computing units.
- CPUs have fewer cores and more control; GPUs have many cores (called SMs) for parallel work.

Important Notes:
- Because CPUs stopped getting much faster, we rely more on parallel computing with GPUs.
- GPUs run many threads that do the same instructions on different data at the same time.

discussions.title: GPU Architecture: Streaming Multiprocessors and Threads
discussions.summary:
Learn how a GPU is built from many streaming multiprocessors (SMs) that control many smaller computing units (SPs), which process many threads in parallel.

Definitions:
- Streaming Multiprocessor (SM): A core unit in a GPU that controls many small processors and manages threads.
- Streaming Processor (SP): A smaller computing unit inside an SM that does calculations on threads.
- Threads: Individual small tasks executed in parallel by SPs.
- Blocks: Groups of threads assigned to one SM.
- Warps: Groups of 32 threads that run one instruction simultaneously.

Key Concepts:
- Each SM controls many SPs and runs many threads in groups called warps.
- Blocks are assigned to SMs to process data in parallel.
- Threads in a warp execute the same instruction on different data, making GPUs efficient for parallel tasks.

Important Notes:
- Understanding blocks, warps, and threads is important for writing efficient GPU programs.
- GPUs have different memory levels with varying speeds; using fast memory near SMs improves performance.

discussions.title: Memory Hierarchy on a GPU and Its Importance
discussions.summary:
This discussion explains the GPU's memory layers, why fast memory near computing units matters, and how slow global memory can limit performance.

Definitions:
- Registers: Very fast memory used by individual threads to store data.
- Shared Memory / L1 Cache: Fast memory shared by threads in the same SM.
- L2 Cache: Slower than shared memory but closer than global memory.
- Global Memory (DRAM): Large but slow memory outside the GPU chip.
- Memory Hierarchy: Different types of memory arranged by speed and distance from the compute unit.

Key Concepts:
- Memory speed matters: closer memory means faster access, which is critical for GPU speed.
- Accessing global memory is slow and can cause the GPU to idle if data is not ready.
- Good programming uses shared memory to keep frequently accessed data close to compute units.
- The physical layout of the GPU affects how fast memory can be accessed.

Important Notes:
- Optimizing memory use is key to good GPU performance.
- Programs should minimize slow global memory use and maximize fast shared memory use.

discussions.title: Brief Overview of TPUs and Comparisons to GPUs
discussions.summary:
This part briefly describes TPUs, another type of hardware accelerator similar to GPUs but simpler in design, focusing mainly on matrix multiplication.

Definitions:
- TPU (Tensor Processing Unit): A specialized chip designed primarily to do matrix multiplications quickly for machine learning.
- Tensor Core: A main working unit in TPUs that performs matrix multiply operations.
- MXU (Matrix Multiply Unit): The hardware part inside TPUs dedicated to fast matrix multiplication.

Key Concepts:
- TPUs have a similar structure to GPUs with fast memory and compute units but are more specialized.
- TPUs focus only on matrix multiplication, making them simpler but efficient for that purpose.
- Understanding GPUs helps in understanding TPUs conceptually.

Important Notes:
- TPUs may not be as flexible as GPUs but are highly optimized for deep learning tasks involving matrices.

discussions.title: GPU Scaling and CUDA Programming Model
discussions.summary:
This section covers why GPUs scale well by adding SMs instead of going faster, introduces CUDA programming basics, and explains threads and instruction execution.

Definitions:
- CUDA Kernel: A function written to run on the GPU that executes many threads in parallel.
- ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit): The part of the processor that performs math operations.
- Lightweight Threads: Threads that use little state and can be easily stopped and started.

Key Concepts:
- GPUs can add more SMs (cores) to scale performance without needing faster clock speeds.
- CUDA programming model runs many threads executing the same instructions on different data.
- Threads are lightweight to allow easy switching and high utilization.

Important Notes:
- GPU is highly parallel with many cores running similar instructions.
- Understanding thread execution and lightweight nature is important for programming efficiently.

discussions.title: History of GPUs and the Special Role of Matrix Multiplication
discussions.summary:
This part talks about the evolution of GPUs from graphics to fast compute devices, especially for matrix multiplication, which is vital for deep learning.

Definitions:
- Matrix Multiply (MatMul): A key operation multiplying two matrices, widely used in neural networks.
- Tensor Cores: Specialized hardware in GPUs that speed up matrix multiplication.
- FLOPS (Floating Point Operations Per Second): A measure of computing speed.

Key Concepts:
- Early GPUs were graphics-focused but were adapted for fast matrix multiplication.
- NVIDIA introduced tensor cores to dramatically speed up matrix multiplies starting with V100 GPUs.
- Most neural network work relies heavily on matrix multiplies because they run much faster on GPUs.

Important Notes:
- Neural architectures should maximize use of matrix multiplication for speed.
- Operations other than matrix multiply run much slower, so they should be minimized.

discussions.title: GPU Performance Scaling: Computation vs Memory and Communication
discussions.summary:
This discusses how GPU compute performance has increased much faster than memory and data communication speeds, making memory the main bottleneck.

Definitions:
- PCIe / NVLink: Types of connections that link GPUs to the host computer.
- Bandwidth: The amount of data that can be moved per second.
- Compute Scaling: How much faster the compute capacity (FLOPS) grows over time.

Key Concepts:
- Compute power of GPUs has scaled super-exponentially over recent years.
- Memory bandwidth and interconnect speeds have grown slower than compute power.
- This creates a bottleneck where memory access limits overall speed.

Important Notes:
- For fast GPU programs, reducing memory use is crucial.
- Future improvements will need better memory efficiency, not just faster compute.

discussions.title: Key GPU Characteristics Recap
discussions.summary:
A quick recap of GPUs being massively parallel, with fast matrix multiplication and a memory hierarchy that must be exploited for performance.

Definitions:
- Memory Hierarchy: The arrangement of memory from fastest/closest to slowest/farthest.

Key Concepts:
- GPUs use many cores (SMs) running the same instructions across threads.
- Matrix multiply has dramatically improved and outpaces other operations.
- Fast memory near the compute units must be used carefully to avoid slowdowns.

Important Notes:
- Remember these facts to understand GPU performance better.

discussions.title: Understanding GPU Performance with the Roofline Model
discussions.summary:
This section explains the roofline model, which shows GPU performance limits from memory bandwidth or compute capacity, and introduces common factors affecting speed.

Definitions:
- Roofline Model: A simple graph explaining GPU performance limited by either memory access or computation.
- Arithmetic Intensity: The amount of compute done per byte of memory accessed.

Key Concepts:
- For small matrix sizes, GPU speed is limited by memory access (memory bound).
- For large matrices, GPU speed is limited by compute units working full time (compute bound).
- We want to operate in the compute-bound region to maximize GPU utilization.

Important Notes:
- Avoid unnecessary memory accesses to prevent slowdowns.
- Seven common GPU performance tips will be discussed, starting with conditionals.

discussions.title: GPU Execution Model and Conditional Branching
discussions.summary:
Learn about GPU’s SIMT (single instruction, multiple threads) model and why conditional statements hurt performance.

Definitions:
- SIMT (Single Instruction Multiple Threads): GPU model where all threads in a warp run the same instruction but on different data.
- Warp: A group of 32 threads that execute together.

Key Concepts:
- When threads in a warp run different instructions due to conditionals, GPUs must run sections sequentially, causing delays.
- Conditional branching in warps reduces performance and should be avoided in GPU programming.

Important Notes:
- Avoid using if-else conditions that split threads within a warp.
- Branch divergence causes GPU threads to idle while others execute.

discussions.title: Reducing Memory Use with Lower Precision
discussions.summary:
Discover how using lower precision numbers (like FP16 instead of FP32) cuts memory bandwidth needs and speeds up GPU computations.

Definitions:
- FP32: 32-bit floating point precision, standard accuracy.
- FP16: 16-bit floating point precision, uses less memory but may reduce accuracy.
- Mixed Precision: Using a mix of high and low precision in different parts of a computation.

Key Concepts:
- Lower precision halves the memory needed for data, doubling throughput if memory is bottleneck.
- For matrix multiplies, use low precision for inputs but higher precision (FP32) for accumulation to keep accuracy.
- Some operations (e.g., with large ranges) may need higher precision like BF16 for stability.

Important Notes:
- Using lower precision improves speed but requires care to keep model stable during training.
- Mixed precision training balances speed and accuracy.

discussions.title: Operator Fusion to Minimize Memory Transfers
discussions.summary:
This part explains operator fusion: combining several separate GPU operations into one to avoid slow memory reads and writes.

Definitions:
- Operator Fusion: Merging multiple GPU operations into a single kernel to reduce memory access.
- Kernel Launch: Running a function on the GPU.

Key Concepts:
- Naive GPU code writes intermediate results to global memory between operations, causing slowdowns.
- Fusing operations keeps intermediate data in fast registers or shared memory within one GPU kernel.
- By fusing, data moves less between computations, greatly improving performance.

Important Notes:
- Modern tools like Torch Compile can automatically fuse operations for you.
- Always try to fuse simple consecutive operations to reduce global memory traffic.

discussions.title: Recomputation: Trading Compute for Memory Bandwidth
discussions.summary:
Recomputation means recalculating values instead of storing and fetching them from slow memory, trading extra compute for less memory use.

Definitions:
- Recomputation: Recalculating intermediate values during backward passes instead of saving them.
- Arithmetic Intensity: Ratio of computations to memory accesses.

Key Concepts:
- During backpropagation, storing activations uses a lot of memory bandwidth.
- Recomputation recalculates activations as needed, saving memory but using more compute.
- This trade-off is good when memory access limits speed.

Important Notes:
- Recomputation is similar to gradient checkpointing but used here to improve speed, not just memory usage.
- Consider recomputation when memory bandwidth is the main bottleneck.

discussions.title: GPU Memory Burst Mode and Coalesced Accesses
discussions.summary:
Learn how GPU memory accesses are faster when threads access adjacent data in bursts, and why organizing accesses properly improves speed.

Definitions:
- Burst Mode: GPU memory returns a block of sequential data when reading one location, making bulk reads faster.
- Memory Coalescing: Grouping threads’ memory accesses so they fall within the same burst block for efficiency.

Key Concepts:
- Reading data in a scattered or random pattern causes many slow memory accesses.
- When all threads in a warp access nearby data in the same burst block, memory efficiency improves up to 4 times.
- Access patterns should be designed to allow coalesced reads.

Important Notes:
- Poorly aligned memory access causes big slowdowns.
- Accessing matrix data by columns or rows affects whether memory accesses are coalesced.

discussions.title: Tiling to Reduce Global Memory Accesses in Matrix Multiplication
discussions.summary:
Tiling breaks matrices into smaller blocks loaded into fast shared memory to minimize slow global memory accesses during multiplication.

Definitions:
- Tiling: Dividing large data into smaller blocks (tiles) processed fully in fast memory before moving to next tile.
- Shared Memory: Fast memory close to compute units, used to store tiles during computation.

Key Concepts:
- Without tiling, the same data is read many times from slow global memory, wasting time.
- With tiling, you load blocks once into shared memory, do many calculations, then move on.
- This reduces global memory reads by a factor equal to tile size.

Important Notes:
- Tiles must fit into shared memory, limiting size.
- Proper tiling and memory access patterns greatly improve performance.

discussions.title: Tiling Size and Alignment Challenges
discussions.summary:
This part highlights problems with tiles that don’t perfectly divide matrix sizes and the importance of alignment with memory burst sections.

Definitions:
- Tile Size: Dimension of a tile, usually a fixed number like 128.
- Wave Quantization: Performance drops when number of tiles poorly matches the number of SMs due to uneven workload.
- Padding: Adding extra elements so the matrix size fits nicely with tiles and memory units.

Key Concepts:
- Tiles that don’t fit evenly cause some SMs to do less work, reducing GPU utilization.
- Memory burst sections must align with tiles to avoid unnecessary extra reads.
- Adding padding to matrix size can fix alignment and speed problems.

Important Notes:
- Choose tile sizes carefully to avoid underused compute resources.
- Alignment and padding can cause large speedups unexpectedly.

discussions.title: Analyzing Performance Weirdness via Tile and SM Counts
discussions.summary:
Explains sudden drops in GPU speed when matrix sizes cause the number of tiles to exceed the number of SMs, forcing sequential execution.

Definitions:
- SM (Streaming Multiprocessor): GPU compute core that executes blocks.
- Tiles: Blocks of the matrix assigned to SMs for processing.

Key Concepts:
- If there are more tiles than SMs, some tiles must wait for others to finish, causing idle SMs and slower execution.
- Increasing matrix size by a small amount can increase tile count above SM count, causing big slowdowns.
- It’s best for tile count to be less than or equal to the number of SMs for full utilization.

Important Notes:
- Monitor tile counts relative to SM counts to avoid performance cliffs.
- Matching tiles and SMs avoids wave quantization losses.

discussions.title: Summary of GPU Performance Tricks
discussions.summary:
A recap of main strategies to improve GPU performance, focusing on reducing memory and improving compute efficiency.

Key Concepts:
- Reduce memory accesses by using coalescing, operator fusion, and moving data to fast shared memory.
- Use tiling to batch computations and minimize global memory use.
- Trade memory use for more compute via recomputation.
- Use lower numerical precision to reduce memory bandwidth.

Important Notes:
- Memory movement is often the main bottleneck, so optimize data flow carefully.
- Understanding hardware details and these strategies leads to faster models.

discussions.title: Flash Attention: Bringing All GPU Techniques Together
discussions.summary:
Flash Attention is a GPU algorithm that accelerates transformer attention by combining tiling, recomputation, and efficient memory use.

Definitions:
- Flash Attention: A fast GPU implementation of transformer attention that uses tiling and recomputation to reduce memory usage.
- Subquadratic HBM Accesses: Having less than quadratic memory access in high bandwidth memory for large matrices.

Key Concepts:
- Flash Attention breaks the attention matrix into tiles to avoid large memory use.
- It uses an online softmax algorithm to compute softmax values incrementally within tiles.
- This reduces memory needed while keeping computations accurate and efficient.

Important Notes:
- Flash Attention hides complexity by optimizing every step: matrix multiplies, softmax, and data movement.
- Recombination and tiling lower the memory bottlenecks that would otherwise slow attention.

discussions.title: Computing Softmax Efficiently Using Online Algorithms in Flash Attention
discussions.summary:
Discusses how Flash Attention computes the softmax part efficiently using an online method that processes data tile by tile without storing the whole matrix.

Definitions:
- Softmax: A function that converts numbers into probabilities by exponentiating and normalizing.
- Online Softmax: A method that incrementally computes softmax terms for streaming data, without needing all data at once.

Key Concepts:
- Softmax normally requires summing over entire rows, which is expensive in memory.
- The online softmax tracks running max and sums to compute softmax piecewise on tiles.
- This approach avoids having to store or process the full attention matrix all at once.

Important Notes:
- You must process all tiles first before final softmax values can be output.
- This online approach reduces memory drastically while allowing accurate softmax computation.

discussions.title: Flash Attention Backward Pass and Recomputation
discussions.summary:
The backward pass recomputes intermediate results tile by tile instead of storing large softmax matrices, saving large memory usage.

Definitions:
- Backward Pass: Computing gradients during training for neural network updates.

Key Concepts:
- Storing full softmax outputs in memory for backward pass is prohibitively large (n squared size).
- Flash Attention recomputes activations as needed during backward pass, same as recomputation technique.

Important Notes:
- Recomputation again trades compute power for memory savings.
- This makes training large transformers memory feasible without losing speed.

discussions.title: Lecture Summary and Importance of Understanding GPU Details
discussions.summary:
The closing section stresses that hardware knowledge is key to advancing large language models and that optimizing memory movement is the main challenge.

Key Concepts:
- Many modern improvements in ML come from understanding and carefully managing GPU hardware details.
- Memory movement often limits GPU performance more than raw compute power.
- Efficient data movement to fast memory leads to better-performance algorithms like Flash Attention.

Important Notes:
- Focus on reducing global memory traffic and maximize use of fast shared memory.
- Hardware understanding empowers building fast new models and algorithms.
